section.title: Daily and Festival Offerings
section.sectionRationale: As Israel prepares to leave the wilderness rhythm behind and settle in the land, God re-establishes the rhythm of worship that must continue there: daily offerings, weekly Sabbath offerings, monthly new moon offerings, and the great annual feasts. Settlement does not mean the end of dependence on God's appointed worship; it means carrying it into a new home.
section.thesis: The LORD commands Israel to keep a continual rhythm of offerings, daily morning and evening, weekly on the Sabbath, monthly at the new moon, and at the yearly feasts of Passover, Unleavened Bread, and Firstfruits, each a pleasing aroma to the LORD, sustaining Israel's worship life once they enter and settle the land.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the daily burnt offering morning and evening; the added offerings for the Sabbath and the new moon; the Passover and the seven days of Unleavened Bread; and the day of firstfruits at the Feast of Weeks.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Command the Israelites and say to them: See that you present to Me at its appointed time the food for My offerings by fire, as a {{aroma|pleasing aroma}} to Me.
And tell them that this is the offering made by fire you are to present to the LORD as a regular burnt offering each day: two unblemished year-old male lambs.
Offer one lamb in the morning and the other at twilight,
along with a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a grain offering, mixed with a quarter hin of oil from pressed olives.
This is a regular burnt offering established at Mount Sinai as a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.
The drink offering accompanying each lamb shall be a quarter hin. Pour out the offering of fermented drink to the LORD in the sanctuary area.
And offer the second lamb at twilight, with the same grain offering and drink offering as in the morning. It is an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

units.label: The Sabbath and new moon offerings
units.note: in addition to the regular burnt offering

verses.text: On the Sabbath day, present two unblemished year-old male lambs, accompanied by a grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil, as well as a drink offering.
This is the burnt offering for every Sabbath, in addition to the regular burnt offering and its drink offering.
At the beginning of every month, you are to present to the LORD a burnt offering of two young bulls, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old, all unblemished,
along with three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering with each bull, two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering with the ram,
and a tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil as a grain offering with each lamb. This is a burnt offering, a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.

Their drink offerings shall be half a hin of wine with each bull, a third of a hin with the ram, and a quarter hin with each lamb. This is the monthly burnt offering to be made at each new moon throughout the year.
In addition to the regular burnt offering with its drink offering, one male goat is to be presented to the LORD as a sin offering.
The fourteenth day of the first month is the LORD's Passover.
On the fifteenth day of this month, there shall be a feast; for seven days unleavened bread is to be eaten.
On the first day there is to be a sacred assembly; you must not do any regular work.
Present to the LORD an offering made by fire, a burnt offering of two young bulls, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old, all unblemished.
The grain offering shall consist of fine flour mixed with oil; offer three-tenths of an ephah with each bull, two-tenths of an ephah with the ram,
and a tenth of an ephah with each of the seven lambs.
Include one male goat as a sin offering to make {{atonement|atonement}} for you.
You are to present these in addition to the regular morning burnt offering.
Offer the same food each day for seven days as an offering made by fire, a pleasing aroma to the LORD. It is to be offered with its drink offering and the regular burnt offering.
On the seventh day you shall hold a sacred assembly; you must not do any regular work.
On the day of firstfruits, when you present an offering of new grain to the LORD during the Feast of Weeks, you are to hold a sacred assembly; you must not do any regular work.
Present a burnt offering of two young bulls, one ram, and seven male lambs a year old as a pleasing aroma to the LORD,
together with their grain offerings of fine flour mixed with oil, three-tenths of an ephah with each bull, two-tenths of an ephah with the ram,
and a tenth of an ephah with each of the seven lambs.
Include one male goat to make atonement for you.
Offer them with their drink offerings in addition to the regular burnt offering and its grain offering. The animals must be unblemished.

aroma.gloss: A pleasing, soothing aroma. The phrase repeats throughout this chapter, describing how faithful, obedient worship is received by God, not because He needs the smoke of a sacrifice but because it represents the costly, fragrant devotion of a heart given to Him.

atonement.gloss: To atone, to cover, to make amends for sin. The sin offering accompanying each festival provides kaphar, atonement, reminding Israel that even joyful worship at appointed feasts still requires a covering for sin before it can be rightly offered.

basic.context: As Israel stands ready to leave the wilderness and enter the land, God lays out the rhythm of worship that must continue there: daily offerings morning and evening, extra offerings each Sabbath and new moon, and the greater festivals of Passover, Unleavened Bread, and the Feast of Weeks. Settling in the land does not mean settling into casual worship.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, detailed set of ceremonial laws for Israel's sacrificial system, not primarily moral instruction for readers today. Its purpose is to establish a sustained, disciplined rhythm of worship, layered daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly, that would carry Israel's dependence on God's provision from the wilderness into permanent settlement.
basic.originalLanguages: Each offering is called a reach nichoach, a pleasing aroma, language repeated throughout the chapter to describe worship that pleases God when offered in faithful obedience. Alongside nearly every festival offering stands a sin offering to make kaphar, atonement, showing that even Israel's most joyful national celebrations required a covering for sin before God.
basic.typology: The unblemished lambs offered morning and evening, without fail, every single day of the year, anticipate the perfect, once-for-all sacrifice of Christ, the Lamb of God, whose single offering ends the need for this daily repetition, Hebrews 10:11-14.
basic.christ: Hebrews 10 explicitly contrasts the daily, repeated offerings of this system, offered again and again yet never able to take away sins, with the single sacrifice of Christ, who by one offering has perfected forever those who are being sanctified; every unblemished lamb offered morning and evening here points toward the spotless Lamb who was offered once, completely.
basic.god: God cares about the rhythm of worship, not leaving it to spontaneous whim but establishing a fixed, generous structure that keeps His people returning to Him daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly. His holiness requires a sin offering even at times of festival joy, yet His provision of that offering shows His readiness to keep His people close to Him.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 10:1-14 contrasts these repeated offerings with Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice. John 1:29 names Jesus the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, echoing the unblemished lambs of this chapter. 1 Corinthians 5:7 calls Christ our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us, drawing directly on the festival named here.
basic.oneStory: Israel's worship life was structured around constant, repeated offerings, daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly, each requiring an unblemished sacrifice and, often, atonement for sin. That entire structure, repeated because it could never finally remove sin, points forward to the one, sufficient sacrifice of Christ, who fulfills and ends the need for endless repetition.

context.literary: The chapter moves in ascending scale, from the smallest daily unit of worship to the largest annual festival, establishing a comprehensive rhythm rather than a single isolated command.
context.historical: Given on the plains of Moab just before entering the land, this chapter functions as a transitional instruction, carrying forward the sacrificial system established at Sinai into the settled agricultural life Israel is about to begin, where new grain and firstfruits will now be genuinely harvested rather than merely anticipated.
context.canonical: This offering calendar becomes foundational to Israel's ongoing temple worship for centuries, referenced and expanded in later texts such as Ezra 3:5 and Nehemiah 10:33, and it forms the backdrop against which the book of Hebrews later explains the superiority of Christ's single sacrifice.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish a comprehensive, sustained rhythm of worship for Israel's life in the land, ensuring dependence on God's provision continues in permanent settlement just as it did in the wilderness.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This chapter is ceremonial law specific to Israel's sacrificial system centered on the tabernacle and later temple; it does not prescribe animal sacrifice for believers today. Its abiding principle, that worship deserves regular, costly, wholehearted devotion, carries forward, fulfilled rather than repeated in Christ's sacrifice and in the ongoing worship of the church.

debates.question: Why does nearly every festival offering include a sin offering even at joyful celebrations?

views.label: Sin offerings maintained ongoing purification necessary for any approach to a holy God
views.case: Even joyful national celebrations occurred in the presence of a holy God, and Israel's persistent tendency toward sin meant that every approach, festive or otherwise, required a fresh covering to maintain the community's fitness to worship.

views.label: The offerings taught that celebration and repentance are not opposites
views.case: By pairing joy and atonement in the very same festival instructions, the law teaches Israel that genuine gladness before God flows from being rightly covered for sin, not from ignoring it, a pattern that shapes healthy worship in every era.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same practical truth Hebrews later draws out: repeated sin offerings, even at times of joy, could never finally remove sin, and this very repetition points forward to the need for, and the sufficiency of, Christ's single sacrifice, which ends the need for ongoing atonement offerings altogether.

study.originalLanguages: Each offering rises as a reach nichoach, pleasing aroma, to the LORD, language used deliberately across every layer of this calendar, daily, weekly, monthly, and festival offerings alike. The recurring sin offering for kaphar, atonement, even at joyful festivals, shows that celebration before a holy God was never separated from the need for covering sin.

typology.type: Unblemished lambs offered without fail, morning and evening, every day
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the spotless Lamb of God, whose single sacrifice ends the need for this daily repetition, Hebrews 10:11-14, John 1:29.

typology.type: The Passover lamb of the fourteenth day
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us once for all, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

study.christ: Hebrews 10:11-14 draws directly on the pattern of this chapter: every priest stands day after day performing his religious duties and offering the same sacrifices again and again, sacrifices that can never take away sins, but Christ offered one sacrifice for sins for all time and has perfected forever those who are being sanctified. The unblemished lambs offered here morning and evening without fail, and the Passover lamb of verse 16, both find their fulfillment in Jesus, the true Lamb of God and our Passover, sacrificed once and completely for us.
study.god: God structures His people's worship with careful, generous regularity, establishing a rhythm that keeps them returning to Him constantly rather than sporadically. His holiness requires atonement even in seasons of festival gladness, yet His provision of that atonement, again and again, shows a God determined to keep His people near, even while that provision remained incomplete until Christ.

crossReferences.note: Contrasts these repeated daily and festival offerings with the single, sufficient sacrifice of Christ.
Names Jesus the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world, echoing the unblemished lambs central to this chapter.
Calls Christ our Passover lamb, sacrificed for us, drawing directly on the festival named in verse 16.
Records the returned exiles resuming this very sacrificial calendar after rebuilding the altar, showing its lasting importance in Israel's worship.

study.oneStory: Israel's worship life was structured around an unending rhythm of offerings, daily, weekly, monthly, and at the great feasts, each pointing to the ongoing need for atonement even amid genuine joy. That entire structure, faithful but never finally sufficient, points forward to Christ, the true Lamb offered once for all, whose single sacrifice fulfills and ends the endless repetition this chapter requires.

quiz.q: How often was the regular burnt offering of two lambs to be presented, according to verses 3 to 4?

options.text: Every day, one lamb in the morning and one at twilight.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 state this daily rhythm exactly.

options.text: Only once a week, on the Sabbath.
options.note: The Sabbath offering was an addition to, not a replacement for, this daily offering.

options.text: Only once a year, at Passover.
options.note: The daily offering continued every single day, distinct from the annual Passover observance.

options.text: No regular schedule is given.
options.note: Verses 3 to 4 give a precise daily schedule.

quiz.q: What was added to the regular burnt offering on the Sabbath, according to verses 9 to 10?

options.text: Two additional unblemished year-old male lambs with their grain and drink offerings.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 specify this addition on top of the daily offering.

options.text: A complete rest from any offerings at all.
options.note: The Sabbath actually required an additional offering, not a suspension of offerings.

options.text: A single bull offered instead of the usual lambs.
options.note: The text specifies two lambs added for the Sabbath, not a substitution with a bull.

options.text: No addition is specified for the Sabbath.
options.note: Verses 9 to 10 specify the addition in detail.

quiz.q: How many days was the Feast of Unleavened Bread to be observed, according to verse 17?

options.text: Seven days.
options.note: Verse 17 states this length directly.

options.text: Three days.
options.note: The text specifies seven days, not three.

options.text: Fourteen days.
options.note: Fourteen is the date of Passover itself, verse 16, not the length of the following feast.

options.text: No length is specified.
options.note: Verse 17 gives the exact length.

quiz.q: What accompanied the burnt offerings at both the Feast of Unleavened Bread and the Feast of Weeks, according to verses 22 and 30?

options.text: A male goat as a sin offering to make atonement.
options.note: Both verses 22 and 30 specify this sin offering for atonement.

options.text: An additional lamb offered at midnight.
options.note: The text specifies a male goat for a sin offering, not an additional midnight lamb.

options.text: No additional offering is mentioned.
options.note: Both verses explicitly mention the added sin offering.

options.text: A drink offering of water rather than wine.
options.note: Drink offerings throughout this chapter are specified as wine, not water.

quiz.q: What common phrase describes the effect of these burnt offerings throughout the chapter?

options.text: A pleasing aroma to the LORD.
options.note: This phrase recurs repeatedly throughout the chapter, for example verses 2, 6, 8, 13, 24, and 27.

options.text: A burden too heavy for the people to bear.
options.note: The chapter's repeated description is positive, a pleasing aroma, not a burden.

options.text: A sign of coming judgment.
options.note: The offerings are described as pleasing to the LORD, not as signs of judgment.

options.text: No description of their effect is given.
options.note: The phrase pleasing aroma appears repeatedly throughout the chapter.

questions: Israel's worship was structured around daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly rhythms, not left to spontaneous impulse. What regular rhythms of worship shape your own life with God right now?

Even Israel's most joyful festivals included an offering for atonement. Where might you need to bring honest confession into a season of celebration rather than assuming joy and repentance cannot coexist?
The daily lamb offering never lapsed, morning and evening, without fail, for centuries. What would it look like for your own devotion to God to have that same unwavering consistency?
Hebrews contrasts these endlessly repeated sacrifices with Christ's single, sufficient offering. Where are you still trying to repeatedly earn what Christ has already accomplished once for all?

This whole calendar of offerings was preparing Israel to worship faithfully once they were settled and comfortable in the land. What practices keep your own worship from growing careless once life becomes comfortable?

translationNotes.note: An ephah and a hin are ancient dry and liquid measures respectively; a tenth of an ephah of flour and a quarter hin of oil represent modest, carefully fixed quantities rather than open-ended amounts, ensuring consistency across every offering.

Fermented drink translates a Hebrew term for strong drink poured out as a drink offering; unlike offerings eaten or burned, this liquid offering was entirely poured out, symbolizing a gift wholly given with nothing kept back.

The LORD's Passover and the following Feast of Unleavened Bread, though distinct in origin, Exodus 12, are treated here as a combined eight-day observance, reflecting how closely the two had become joined in Israel's actual practice by this point.
